Shivani Aggarwal · Lead Architect · Fractal AI · Abu Dhabi
About

A rare intersection of depth and breadth.

The current work sits at the centre of Abu Dhabi's national AI ambition. As Lead Data & AI Architect at Fractal AI, the role involves architecting the Population Health Intelligence platform — a Microsoft-partnered initiative that integrates clinical, lifestyle, and environmental signals across an entire emirate's population. As the UAE moves to become the world's first AI-native government, this is the kind of infrastructure that makes that possible. Very few data professionals globally have built at this level.

The path here wasn't linear — it was deliberate. Infosys → EY LLP → Fractal Analytics → Fractal AI across India, Belgium, and the UAE — each move chosen for depth, not comfort. At EY, eight enterprise projects delivered end-to-end with no handoffs: NLP pipelines for Fidelity International, SASB-aligned carbon intelligence for AB InBev and Castrol, ML deployed in Belgium at ArcelorMittal. The discipline formed there — owning every layer from architecture through go-live — is the one that shows up on every engagement since.

What makes this profile genuinely unusual is what sits above the technical layer. A CFA Level I credential means financial analysis is native, not translated — data platforms get positioned in ROI terms, ESG carries real accounting rigour, and AI governance lands with regulatory precision. Add original research on AI taxation law and carbon modelling as fiscal strategy, and the result is someone who doesn't just build the system — they understand exactly what it costs, what it's worth, and how to defend it in the boardroom.

✦ What I bring that's genuinely rare
Architecting national-scale AI in Abu Dhabi as the UAE builds toward the world's first AI-native government — very few practitioners globally have worked at this level
The only profile combining: Databricks Architect + CFA Level I + published AI taxation research + GCC government delivery
Proven cross-domain depth across healthcare, ESG, finance, manufacturing, and aviation — not generalism, but genuine pattern recognition across high-stakes verticals
UAE residency, Abu Dhabi government relationships, and in-country delivery track record — ready to contribute from day one
Full lifecycle ownership every time — the architect who can walk into a boardroom and a Databricks cluster on the same morning

Project Portfolio

EY LLP · 2017–2022 · 5 Years · Selected Projects

Five years at one of the world's most prestigious firms — independently delivering end-to-end data & AI projects across Finance, ESG, Manufacturing, Government, and Aviation. NLP, OCR, ESG analytics, and RPA automation — all while representing EY internationally in Belgium.

Data Governance Architecture

Building governance that makes data trustworthy, traceable, and secure — from source to AI layer.

🏛️
Unity Catalog
Centralized access control, lineage, and audit logging across Databricks workspaces. Column and row-level security aligned to SOC standards.
🔍
Microsoft Purview
Data discovery, classification, and lineage mapping across the Azure estate. Automated sensitivity labeling and compliance reporting for PHI/PII data.
🔐
PHI/PII Masking
Led masking on Databricks views using SOC-approved column lists and AAD group-level access controls for DoH Abu Dhabi health records.
🌊
Data Lineage
End-to-end lineage from source through medallion layers to BI — enabling impact analysis and regulatory traceability at national scale.
👥
RBAC & AAD Integration
Azure Active Directory role management, service principal governance, and workspace access policies across enterprise Databricks environments.
📋
Data Quality & SLAs
DLT expectations, validation pipelines, and monitoring dashboards enforcing data SLAs across production healthcare medallion layers.

Thought Leadership

Research & Writing

Exploring the policy, tax, and governance implications of AI systems I've actually built — where implementation experience meets regulatory analysis.

ESG · Carbon Policy · Tax 2026 · Working Paper

Predictive Carbon Modelling as a Strategic Tax Governance Tool

Reconciling Industrial Decarbonization with Fiscal Resilience

The EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism has transformed carbon accounting from a voluntary ESG disclosure exercise into a direct tax liability. This paper argues that predictive carbon modelling — integrating COGS data, supply chain emissions, and shadow carbon pricing — has become a cornerstone of modern tax governance, not merely a compliance tool. Drawing on a real implementation for a major lubricants manufacturer, the paper demonstrates how a four-layer carbon model can reshape capital allocation, supply chain configuration, and Pillar Two interaction strategies.

Key Arguments
Carbon taxation is tethered to physical reality — unlike profit, emissions cannot be booked into a lower-tax jurisdiction through transfer pricing structures.
A €2M renewable heat investment with a 7-year conventional payback drops to 3.8 years when CBAM liabilities and shadow carbon pricing are incorporated — materially changing the capital decision.
Sourcing base oils from low-carbon Norwegian refineries vs. conventional Middle Eastern facilities generates a 4–7% post-tax margin improvement at EU export scale.
OECD Pillar Two minimum tax interactions with green investment credits create optimization complexity that requires integrated carbon-tax scenario modelling to navigate.
CBAMSASB · IFRS S2Shadow Carbon PricingPillar TwoSupply Chain Tax
Working Paper · Not Published
Healthcare AI · Tax Policy · UAE January 2026 · Working Paper

Tax Implications of Healthcare Data Localization

Permanent Establishment and Transfer Pricing Challenges in Cloud-Based AI Platforms

Over seventy jurisdictions now mandate local storage and processing of healthcare data. When AI platforms — like Abu Dhabi's Population Health Intelligence system — must train models exclusively within UAE infrastructure while development teams sit offshore, this geographic separation creates novel permanent establishment and transfer pricing questions that current OECD frameworks were not designed to answer. This paper analyses those gaps using direct implementation experience under Abu Dhabi DOH Circular 147/2022.

Key Arguments
Mandated local AI model training creates stronger permanent establishment arguments than traditional automated server operations — the processing is core to business value, not ancillary.
Cost-plus transfer pricing (10–15% markup) systematically undervalues the UAE data processing contribution; profit-split approaches allocating ~40% to local operations may be more defensible.
Market jurisdictions can make credible claims to taxation rights over AI models trained with their citizens' data — a question OECD Pillar One's Amount A does not adequately address.
Policy recommendation: safe harbors where processing is fully automated, compliance-driven, and below revenue thresholds would reduce uncertainty without sacrificing tax base protection.
Permanent EstablishmentTransfer PricingUAE Data SovereigntyOECD Pillar OneAI Taxation
Working Paper · Not Published
Research Interests
AI Taxation & Digital PE Carbon Border Adjustment (CBAM) Healthcare Data Sovereignty Transfer Pricing for AI Development OECD Pillar One & Two ESG as Fiscal Strategy Agentic AI Governance
